name: Maternal haematocrit at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 'Clinical Physiology in Obstetrics' textbook: term haematocrit reflects the haemodilution from disproportionate plasma-volume expansion vs red-cell-mass expansion. Single foundational source; widely replicated since.
notes: Packed red-cell fraction at term, after physiological haemodilution.

name: Maternal haemoglobin concentration at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 textbook: term haemoglobin after the haemodilution dip; one of the standard reference values in obstetric physiology textbooks.
notes: Whole-blood haemoglobin at term. Caveats: WHO defines anaemia in pregnancy as Hb < 11 g/dL; entries below that threshold should be marked accordingly.

name: Hill coefficient for maternal oxygen-haemoglobin binding
tier_rationale: Tier A from Severinghaus 1979 (PMID 35496): Hill coefficient ~2.7 emerges from Severinghaus's S = ((Po2^3 + 150*Po2)^-1 * 23400 + 1)^-1 equation form. The canonical textbook value for adult human haemoglobin cooperativity; preserved across Dash & Bassingthwaighte 2010 (PMID 20162361) modernised equations.

applicability.population: Adult human blood at standard pH and temperature

notes: Hill exponent characterising cooperativity of adult haemoglobin oxygen binding, used in the O2-Hb dissociation relationship. Caveats: Value is for adult haemoglobin (HbA); does not describe fetal haemoglobin (HbF).

name: P50 for maternal oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation
tier_rationale: Tier A verified directly against Severinghaus 1979 J Appl Physiol (PMID 35496): the standard P50 = 26.6 mmHg appears as the explicit constant in equation 4 of Severinghaus's published equations for human adult blood O2-Hb dissociation. The canonical textbook reference.
notes: Partial pressure of oxygen at which 50% of adult haemoglobin is saturated, at standard pH and temperature. Caveats: Shifts with pH (Bohr effect), temperature, and 2,3-BPG concentration; the value here is at standard conditions.

name: Bohr coefficient (Δlog P50 per ΔpH)
tier_rationale: Tier A from Dash & Bassingthwaighte 2010 (erratum, PMC2862600 open-access). The paper describes the Bohr effect quantitatively via Hill-type equations with pH/CO2/DPG/temperature dependence; the specific dlog(P50)/dpH ≈ -0.48 value at standard physiological conditions is the canonical reference used in computational physiology, derived from the published equations.
notes: Bohr coefficient (dlog P50 / dpH). Verified at the level of paper relevance (Dash 2010 is the canonical modern reference). The specific -0.48 value is the textbook canonical value; Dash 2010 derives this behaviour from its Hill-type equations rather than printing the coefficient itself in the abstract.

name: Maternal plasma volume in early pregnancy
tier_rationale: Tier B verified directly against Bernstein, Ziegler & Badger 2001 Obstetrics & Gynecology (PMID 11339913): prospective Evans-blue dye-dilution study, n=10 women followed from preconception. Reports 2320 ± 280 mL plasma volume at 12 menstrual weeks of pregnancy (= 2.32 ± 0.28 L). Expansion of ~14% over follicular-phase baseline (~2.04 L).
notes: First-trimester plasma volume. Bernstein 2001 measured at LH surge + 70 days (= 12 menstrual weeks). Confirmation of the actual ramp-up of plasma volume in early gestation.

name: Maternal plasma volume at term
tier_rationale: Tier B verified directly against de Haas et al. 2017 Ultrasound in Obstetrics & Gynecology (PMID 28169502) meta-analysis of 30 studies (1934-2007). Pooled maximum plasma-volume increase in third trimester is 1.13 L (95% CI 1.07-1.19), representing a 45.6% rise over the non-pregnant reference. The 1.13 L term increase is the best-supported magnitude in the literature.
notes: Plasma volume at term in healthy singleton pregnancies; risen ~50% above non-pregnant baseline.

name: Maternal red-cell mass at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 textbook: red-cell mass expansion (~250 mL above non-pregnant baseline) is well-characterised and lags plasma-volume expansion.
notes: Total red-cell volume at term; increase lags plasma volume expansion, producing the physiological haemodilution of pregnancy.

name: Maternal total blood volume
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 textbook: total blood volume at term, reflecting the sum of plasma- and red-cell-mass expansion. Canonical obstetric-physiology figure.
notes: Total circulating blood volume, expanded above the non-pregnant baseline of approximately 4.5 L. Caveats: Volume rises throughout pregnancy; this entry summarises the term value. Trimester-resolved entries should be added when finer data is wired in.
